title: opentag vs Alternatives: Which Frontend Tool Should You Choose?
description: Compare opentag with popular Frontend alternatives. See features, performance, and community stats.

sourceData.name: opentag
sourceData.description: Open-source @agent mentions for Slack and GitHub. OpenTag routes tagged requests to Codex, Claude Code, then returns results in thread.

bodyHtml:
opentag is making waves in the Frontend ecosystem this week, gaining 5 new stars. With a total of 356 stars on GitHub and 33 forks, this open-source project is rapidly becoming a go-to tool for developers worldwide.

⭐ 356Stars
📈 +5/wkGrowth
🔀 33Forks
⚠️ 5Open Issues

Key Features

  • Written in TypeScript for maximum performance
  • Licensed under MIT — free to use and modify
  • 🏷️ agent-skills
  • 🏷️ ai-agents
  • 🏷️ claude
  • 🏷️ codex
  • 🏷️ github

Why opentag Is Trending

The Frontend landscape is constantly evolving, and opentag has emerged as one of the fastest-growing projects this week. Several factors contribute to its momentum:

  1. Growing community: With 5 new stars this week alone, developer adoption is accelerating rapidly.
  2. Active maintenance: The project sees regular updates, ensuring compatibility with the latest ecosystem changes.
  3. Practical utility: Open-source @agent mentions for Slack and GitHub. OpenTag routes tagged requests to Codex, Claude Code, then returns results in thread.

If you're working in Frontend, this is definitely a project worth watching — and adopting.

How It Compares to Alternatives

When evaluating opentag, it's natural to compare it against established alternatives. Here's what sets it apart:

  • Performance: Built with TypeScript, it leverages modern language features for speed.
  • Community size: 356 stars indicate strong community trust and validation.
  • License: MIT licensing means no commercial restrictions.

Final Thoughts

opentag is a solid addition to any developer's toolkit. With its growing community, active development, and practical design, it's well worth your time to evaluate. Star it on GitHub, try it in a side project, and see if it fits your workflow.
